message.content:
Build the public profile page UI only.

Files need to be created or modify:
- src/app/[handle]/page.tsx

src/app/[handle]/page.tsx
- Find the profile by using handle name in prisma
- If handle not exist then return 404 page
- Get the userId from that profile
- Fetch all bookmarks for that handle where userId=that userId from profile and isPublic=true
- User's handle name should be display at top
- List all bookmarks In cards
- Each cards shows title, url which is clickable and opens in new tab
- If user has no public bookmark then show empty state message
- No edit/delete buttons as this is public view only
- If handle not found then return not Found page

UI requirements:
- Clean minimal light design, white background, no dark colors
- Use shadcn Card component for bookmark cards
- Use lucide-react ExternalLink icon next to each URL
- cursor-pointer on all clickable elements

This is a server component, no authentication required.
Use src/lib/prisma.ts for all database queries.
Do not modify any other files.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app/03-api-reference/04-functions/not-found.md
node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app/03-api-reference/03-file-conventions/not-found.md
node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app/03-api-reference/03-file-conventions/dynamic-routes.md
node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app/03-api-reference/03-file-conventions/02-route-segment-config/dynamicParams.md

1	---
2	title: notFound
3	description: API Reference for the notFound function.
4	---
5	
6	The `notFound` function allows you to render the [`not-found file`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/not-found) within a route segment as well as inject a [`<meta name="robots" content="noindex" />`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/loading#status-codes) tag for search engines.
7	
8	## `notFound()`
9	
10	Invoking the `notFound()` function throws a `NEXT_HTTP_ERROR_FALLBACK;404` error and terminates rendering of the route segment in which it was thrown. Specifying a [**not-found** file](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/not-found) allows you to gracefully handle such errors by rendering a Not Found UI within the segment.
11	
12	```jsx filename="app/user/[id]/page.js"
13	import { notFound } from 'next/navigation'
14	
15	async function fetchUser(id) {
16	  const res = await fetch('https://...')
17	  if (!res.ok) return undefined
18	  return res.json()
19	}
20	
21	export default async function Profile({ params }) {
22	  const { id } = await params
23	  const user = await fetchUser(id)
24	
25	  if (!user) {
26	    notFound()
27	  }
28	
29	  // ...
30	}
31	```
32	
33	> **Good to know**: `notFound()` does not require you to use `return notFound()` due to using the TypeScript [`never`](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/2/functions.html#never) type.
34	
35	## Version History
36	
37	| Version   | Changes                |
38	| --------- | ---------------------- |
39	| `v13.0.0` | `notFound` introduced. |
40

1 --- 2 title: not-found.js 3 description: API reference for the not-found.js file. 4 --- 5 6 Next.js provides two conventions to handle not found cases: 7 8 - **`not-found.js`**: Used when you call the [`notFound`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/not-found) function in a route segment. 9 - **`global-not-found.js`**: Used to define a global 404 page for unmatched routes across your entire app. This is handled at the routing level and doesn't depend on rendering a layout or page. 10 11 ## `not-found.js` 12 13 The **not-found** file is used to render UI when the [`notFound`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/not-found) function is thrown within a route segment. Along with serving a custom UI, Next.js will return a `200` HTTP status code for streamed responses, and `404` for non-streamed responses (see [Status Codes](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/loading#status-codes) for details about SEO). 14 15 ```tsx filename="app/not-found.tsx" switcher 16 import Link from 'next/link' 17 18 export default function NotFound() { 19 return ( 20
21

Not Found

22

Could not find requested resource

23 Return Home 24
25 ) 26 } 27 ``` 28 29 ```jsx filename="app/blog/not-found.js" switcher 30 import Link from 'next/link' 31 32 export default function NotFound() { 33 return ( 34
35

Not Found

36

Could not find requested resource

37 Return Home 38
39 ) 40 } 41 ``` 42 43 In the [component hierarchy](/docs/app/getting-started/project-structure#component-hierarchy), `not-found.js` renders between `loading.js` and `page.js`. It is wrapped by the `` boundary from `loading.js` and the error boundary from `error.js` in the same segment. 44 45 ## `global-not-found.js` (experimental) 46 47 The `global-not-found.js` file lets you define a 404 page for your entire application. Unlike `not-found.js`, which works at the route level, this is used when a requested URL doesn't match any route at all. Next.js **skips rendering** and directly returns this global page. 48 49 The `global-not-found.js` file bypasses your app's normal rendering, which means you'll need to import any global styles, fonts, or other dependencies that your 404 page requires. 50 51 > **Good to know**: A smaller version of your global styles, and a simpler font family could improve performance of this page. 52 53 `global-not-found.js` is useful when you can't build a 404 page using a combination of `layout.js` and `not-found.js`. This can happen in two cases: 54 55 - Your app has multiple root layouts (e.g. `app/(admin)/layout.tsx` and `app/(shop)/layout.tsx`), so there's no single layout to compose a global 404 from. 56 - Your root layout is defined using top-level dynamic segments (e.g. `app/[country]/layout.tsx`), which makes composing a consistent 404 page harder. 57 58 To enable it, add the `globalNotFound` flag in `next.config.ts`: 59 60 ```tsx filename="next.config.ts" 61 import type { NextConfig } from 'next' 62 63 const nextConfig: NextConfig = { 64 experimental: { 65 globalNotFound: true, 66 }, 67 } 68 69 export default nextConfig 70 ``` 71 72 Then, create a file in the root of the `app` directory: `app/global-not-found.js`: 73 74 ```tsx filename="app/global-not-found.tsx" switcher 75 // Import global styles and fonts 76 import './globals.css' 77 import { Inter } from 'next/font/google' 78 import type { Metadata } from 'next' 79 80 const inter = Inter({ subsets: ['latin'] }) 81 82 export const metadata: Metadata = { 83 title: '404 - Page Not Found', 84 description: 'The page you are looking for does not exist.', 85 } 86 87 export default function GlobalNotFound() { 88 return ( 89 90 91

404 - Page Not Found

92

This page does not exist.

93 94 95 ) 96 } 97 ``` 98 99 ```jsx filename="app/global-not-found.js" switcher 100 // Import global styles and fonts 101 import './globals.css' 102 import { Inter } from 'next/font/google' 103 104 const inter = Inter({ subsets: ['latin'] }) 105 106 export const metadata = { 107 title: '404 - Page Not Found', 108 description: 'The page you are looking for does not exist.', 109 } 110 111 export default function GlobalNotFound() { 112 return ( 113 114 115

404 - Page Not Found

116

This page does not exist.

117 118 119 ) 120 } 121 ``` 122 123 Unlike `not-found.js`, this file must return a full HTML document, including `` and `` tags. 124 125 ## Reference 126 127 ### Props 128 129 `not-found.js` or `global-not-found.js` components do not accept any props. 130 131 > **Good to know**: In addition to catching expected `notFound()` errors, the root `app/not-found.js` and `app/global-not-found.js` files handle any unmatched URLs for your whole application. This means users that visit a URL that is not handled by your app will be shown the exported UI. 132 133 ## Examples 134 135 ### Data Fetching 136 137 By default, `not-found` is a Server Component. You can mark it as `async` to fetch and display data: 138 139 ```tsx filename="app/not-found.tsx" switcher 140 import Link from 'next/link' 141 import { headers } from 'next/headers' 142 143 export default async function NotFound() { 144 const headersList = await headers() 145 const domain = headersList.get('host') 146 const data = await getSiteData(domain) 147 return ( 148
149

Not Found: {data.name}

150

Could not find requested resource

151

152 View all posts 153

154
155 ) 156 } 157 ``` 158 159 ```jsx filename="app/not-found.jsx" switcher 160 import Link from 'next/link' 161 import { headers } from 'next/headers' 162 163 export default async function NotFound() { 164 const headersList = await headers() 165 const domain = headersList.get('host') 166 const data = await getSiteData(domain) 167 return ( 168
169

Not Found: {data.name}

170

Could not find requested resource

171

172 View all posts 173

174
175 ) 176 } 177 ``` 178 179 If you need to use Client Component hooks like `usePathname` to display content based on the path, you must fetch data on the client-side instead. 180 181 ### Metadata 182 183 For `global-not-found.js`, you can export a `metadata` object or a [`generateMetadata`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata) function to customize the ``, `<meta>`, and other head tags for your 404 page: 184 185 > **Good to know**: Next.js automatically injects `<meta name="robots" content="noindex" />` for pages that return a 404 status code, including `global-not-found.js` pages. 186 187 ```tsx filename="app/global-not-found.tsx" switcher 188 import type { Metadata } from 'next' 189 190 export const metadata: Metadata = { 191 title: 'Not Found', 192 description: 'The page you are looking for does not exist.', 193 } 194 195 export default function GlobalNotFound() { 196 return ( 197 <html lang="en"> 198 <body> 199 <div> 200 <h1>Not Found</h1> 201 <p>The page you are looking for does not exist.</p> 202 </div> 203 </body> 204 </html> 205 ) 206 } 207 ``` 208 209 ```jsx filename="app/global-not-found.js" switcher 210 export const metadata = { 211 title: 'Not Found', 212 description: 'The page you are looking for does not exist.', 213 } 214 215 export default function GlobalNotFound() { 216 return ( 217 <html lang="en"> 218 <body> 219 <div> 220 <h1>Not Found</h1> 221 <p>The page you are looking for does not exist.</p> 222 </div> 223 </body> 224 </html> 225 ) 226 } 227 ``` 228 229 ## Version History 230 231 | Version | Changes | 232 | --------- | --------------------------------------------------- | 233 | `v15.4.0` | `global-not-found.js` introduced (experimental). | 234 | `v13.3.0` | Root `app/not-found` handles global unmatched URLs. | 235 | `v13.0.0` | `not-found` introduced. | 236

--- title: not-found.js description: API reference for the not-found.js file. --- Next.js provides two conventions to handle not found cases: - **`not-found.js`**: Used when you call the [`notFound`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/not-found) function in a route segment. - **`global-not-found.js`**: Used to define a global 404 page for unmatched routes across your entire app. This is handled at the routing level and doesn't depend on rendering a layout or page. ## `not-found.js` The **not-found** file is used to render UI when the [`notFound`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/not-found) function is thrown within a route segment. Along with serving a custom UI, Next.js will return a `200` HTTP status code for streamed responses, and `404` for non-streamed responses (see [Status Codes](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/loading#status-codes) for details about SEO). ```tsx filename="app/not-found.tsx" switcher import Link from 'next/link' export default function NotFound() { return (

Not Found

Could not find requested resource

Return Home
) } ``` ```jsx filename="app/blog/not-found.js" switcher import Link from 'next/link' export default function NotFound() { return (

Not Found

Could not find requested resource

Return Home
) } ``` In the [component hierarchy](/docs/app/getting-started/project-structure#component-hierarchy), `not-found.js` renders between `loading.js` and `page.js`. It is wrapped by the `` boundary from `loading.js` and the error boundary from `error.js` in the same segment. ## `global-not-found.js` (experimental) The `global-not-found.js` file lets you define a 404 page for your entire application. Unlike `not-found.js`, which works at the route level, this is used when a requested URL doesn't match any route at all. Next.js **skips rendering** and directly returns this global page. The `global-not-found.js` file bypasses your app's normal rendering, which means you'll need to import any global styles, fonts, or other dependencies that your 404 page requires. > **Good to know**: A smaller version of your global styles, and a simpler font family could improve performance of this page. `global-not-found.js` is useful when you can't build a 404 page using a combination of `layout.js` and `not-found.js`. This can happen in two cases: - Your app has multiple root layouts (e.g. `app/(admin)/layout.tsx` and `app/(shop)/layout.tsx`), so there's no single layout to compose a global 404 from. - Your root layout is defined using top-level dynamic segments (e.g. `app/[country]/layout.tsx`), which makes composing a consistent 404 page harder. To enable it, add the `globalNotFound` flag in `next.config.ts`: ```tsx filename="next.config.ts" import type { NextConfig } from 'next' const nextConfig: NextConfig = { experimental: { globalNotFound: true, }, } export default nextConfig ``` Then, create a file in the root of the `app` directory: `app/global-not-found.js`: ```tsx filename="app/global-not-found.tsx" switcher // Import global styles and fonts import './globals.css' import { Inter } from 'next/font/google' import type { Metadata } from 'next' const inter = Inter({ subsets: ['latin'] }) export const metadata: Metadata = { title: '404 - Page Not Found', description: 'The page you are looking for does not exist.', } export default function GlobalNotFound() { return (

404 - Page Not Found

This page does not exist.

) } ``` ```jsx filename="app/global-not-found.js" switcher // Import global styles and fonts import './globals.css' import { Inter } from 'next/font/google' const inter = Inter({ subsets: ['latin'] }) export const metadata = { title: '404 - Page Not Found', description: 'The page you are looking for does not exist.', } export default function GlobalNotFound() { return (

404 - Page Not Found

This page does not exist.

) } ``` Unlike `not-found.js`, this file must return a full HTML document, including `` and `` tags. ## Reference ### Props `not-found.js` or `global-not-found.js` components do not accept any props. > **Good to know**: In addition to catching expected `notFound()` errors, the root `app/not-found.js` and `app/global-not-found.js` files handle any unmatched URLs for your whole application. This means users that visit a URL that is not handled by your app will be shown the exported UI. ## Examples ### Data Fetching By default, `not-found` is a Server Component. You can mark it as `async` to fetch and display data: ```tsx filename="app/not-found.tsx" switcher import Link from 'next/link' import { headers } from 'next/headers' export default async function NotFound() { const headersList = await headers() const domain = headersList.get('host') const data = await getSiteData(domain) return (

Not Found: {data.name}

Could not find requested resource

View all posts

) } ``` ```jsx filename="app/not-found.jsx" switcher import Link from 'next/link' import { headers } from 'next/headers' export default async function NotFound() { const headersList = await headers() const domain = headersList.get('host') const data = await getSiteData(domain) return (

Not Found: {data.name}

Could not find requested resource

View all posts

) } ``` If you need to use Client Component hooks like `usePathname` to display content based on the path, you must fetch data on the client-side instead. ### Metadata For `global-not-found.js`, you can export a `metadata` object or a [`generateMetadata`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata) function to customize the ``, `<meta>`, and other head tags for your 404 page: > **Good to know**: Next.js automatically injects `<meta name="robots" content="noindex" />` for pages that return a 404 status code, including `global-not-found.js` pages. ```tsx filename="app/global-not-found.tsx" switcher import type { Metadata } from 'next' export const metadata: Metadata = { title: 'Not Found', description: 'The page you are looking for does not exist.', } export default function GlobalNotFound() { return ( <html lang="en"> <body> <div> <h1>Not Found</h1> <p>The page you are looking for does not exist.</p> </div> </body> </html> ) } ``` ```jsx filename="app/global-not-found.js" switcher export const metadata = { title: 'Not Found', description: 'The page you are looking for does not exist.', } export default function GlobalNotFound() { return ( <html lang="en"> <body> <div> <h1>Not Found</h1> <p>The page you are looking for does not exist.</p> </div> </body> </html> ) } ``` ## Version History | Version | Changes | | --------- | --------------------------------------------------- | | `v15.4.0` | `global-not-found.js` introduced (experimental). | | `v13.3.0` | Root `app/not-found` handles global unmatched URLs. | | `v13.0.0` | `not-found` introduced. |

content.text:
Type check passes. The public profile page at `src/app/[handle]/page.tsx` is built:

- Looks up `Profile` by `handle` via Prisma; calls `notFound()` if missing (renders the app's 404 via `not-found.js`/global handler).
- Fetches `Bookmark`s for that profile's `userId` where `isPublic: true`, newest first.
- Displays `@handle` as a heading, bookmarks in a responsive grid of shadcn `Card`s (title + clickable URL with `ExternalLink` icon, opens in new tab), and a centered empty-state message when there are no public bookmarks.
- View-only — no edit/delete controls, no auth required, clean white/light styling matching the dashboard.

- Two buttons side by side: "Get Started" links to /signup, "Log In" links to /login
- Centered layout, vertically and horizontally centered on screen
- No dark colors
- Do not modify any other files

7	  "group/button inline-flex shrink-0 items-center justify-center rounded-lg border border-transparent bg-clip-padding text-sm font-medium whitespace-nowrap transition-all outline-none select-none focus-visible:border-ring focus-visible:ring-3 focus-visible:ring-ring/50 active:not-aria-[haspopup]:translate-y-px disabled:pointer-events-none disabled:opacity-50 aria-invalid:border-destructive aria-invalid:ring-3 aria-invalid:ring-destructive/20 dark:aria-invalid:border-destructive/50 dark:aria-invalid:ring-destructive/40 [&_svg]:pointer-events-none [&_svg]:shrink-0 [&_svg:not([class*='size-'])]:size-4",

26	        sm: "h-7 gap-1 rounded-[min(var(--radius-md),12px)] px-2.5 text-[0.8rem] in-data-[slot=button-group]:rounded-lg has-data-[icon=inline-end]:pr-1.5 has-data-[icon=inline-start]:pl-1.5 [&_svg:not([class*='size-'])]:size-3.5",

42	
43	function Button({
44	  className,
45	  variant = "default",
46	  size = "default",
47	  ...props
48	}: ButtonPrimitive.Props & VariantProps<typeof buttonVariants>) {
49	  return (
50	    <ButtonPrimitive
51	      data-slot="button"
52	      className={cn(buttonVariants({ variant, size, className }))}
53	      {...props}
54	    />
55	  )
56	}
57	
58	export { Button, buttonVariants }

content.content: node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app/03-api-reference/04-functions/after.md

1 --- 2 title: after 3 description: API Reference for the after function. 4 --- 5 6 `after` allows you to schedule work to be executed after a response (or prerender) is finished. This is useful for tasks and other side effects that should not block the response, such as logging and analytics. 7 8 It can be used in [Server Components](/docs/app/getting-started/server-and-client-components) (including [`generateMetadata`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata)), [Server Functions](/docs/app/getting-started/mutating-data), [Route Handlers](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route), and [Proxy](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/proxy). 9 10 The function accepts a callback that will be executed after the response (or prerender) is finished: 11 12 ```tsx filename="app/layout.tsx" switcher 13 import { after } from 'next/server' 14 // Custom logging function 15 import { log } from '@/app/utils' 16 17 export default function Layout({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) { 18 after(() => { 19 // Execute after the layout is rendered and sent to the user 20 log() 21 }) 22 return <>{children} 23 } 24 ``` 25 26 ```jsx filename="app/layout.jsx" switcher 27 import { after } from 'next/server' 28 // Custom logging function 29 import { log } from '@/app/utils' 30 31 export default function Layout({ children }) { 32 after(() => { 33 // Execute after the layout is rendered and sent to the user 34 log() 35 }) 36 return <>{children} 37 } 38 ``` 39 40 > **Good to know:** `after` is not a [Request-time API](/docs/app/glossary#request-time-apis) and calling it does not cause a route to become dynamic. If it's used within a static page, the callback will execute at build time, or whenever a page is revalidated. 41 42 ## Reference 43 44 ### Parameters 45 46 - A callback function which will be executed after the response (or prerender) is finished. 47 48 ### Duration 49 50 `after` will run for the platform's default or configured max duration of your route. If your platform supports it, you can configure the timeout limit using the [`maxDuration`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route-segment-config/maxDuration) route segment config. 51 52 ## Good to know 53 54 - `after` will be executed even if the response didn't complete successfully. Including when an error is thrown or when `notFound` or `redirect` is called. 55 - You can use React `cache` to deduplicate functions called inside `after`. 56 - `after` can be nested inside other `after` calls, for example, you can create utility functions that wrap `after` calls to add additional functionality. 57 58 ## Examples 59 60 ### With request APIs 61 62 Whether you can use request APIs like [`cookies`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cookies) and [`headers`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/headers) inside `after` depends on where `after` is called from. 63 64 #### In Route Handlers and Server Functions 65 66 You can call `cookies` and `headers` directly inside the `after` callback when used in [Route Handlers](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route) and [Server Functions](/docs/app/getting-started/mutating-data). This is useful for logging activity after a mutation or API request. For example: 67 68 ```ts filename="app/api/route.ts" highlight={2,10-16} switcher 69 import { after } from 'next/server' 70 import { cookies, headers } from 'next/headers' 71 import { logUserAction } from '@/app/utils' 72 73 export async function POST(request: Request) { 74 // Perform mutation 75 // ... 76 77 // Log user activity for analytics 78 after(async () => { 79 const userAgent = (await headers()).get('user-agent') || 'unknown' 80 const sessionCookie = 81 (await cookies()).get('session-id')?.value || 'anonymous' 82 83 logUserAction({ sessionCookie, userAgent }) 84 }) 85 86 return new Response(JSON.stringify({ status: 'success' }), { 87 status: 200, 88 headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' }, 89 }) 90 } 91 ``` 92 93 ```js filename="app/api/route.js" highlight={2,10-16} switcher 94 import { after } from 'next/server' 95 import { cookies, headers } from 'next/headers' 96 import { logUserAction } from '@/app/utils' 97 98 export async function POST(request) { 99 // Perform mutation 100 // ... 101 102 // Log user activity for analytics 103 after(async () => { 104 const userAgent = (await headers()).get('user-agent') || 'unknown' 105 const sessionCookie = 106 (await cookies()).get('session-id')?.value || 'anonymous' 107 108 logUserAction({ sessionCookie, userAgent }) 109 }) 110 111 return new Response(JSON.stringify({ status: 'success' }), { 112 status: 200, 113 headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' }, 114 }) 115 } 116 ``` 117 118 #### In Server Components (pages and layouts) 119 120 [Server Components](/docs/app/getting-started/server-and-client-components) (including pages, layouts, and `generateMetadata`) **cannot** use `cookies`, `headers`, or other Request-time APIs inside `after`. This is because Next.js needs to know which part of the component tree accesses request data to support [Partial Prerendering](/docs/app/glossary#partial-prerendering-ppr) and [Cache Components](/docs/app/getting-started/caching), but `after` runs after React's rendering lifecycle. 121 122 If you need request data inside an `after` callback in a Server Component, read it beforehand and pass the values in: 123 124 ```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" highlight={8-10,12} switcher 125 import { after } from 'next/server' 126 import { cookies, headers } from 'next/headers' 127 import { logUserAction } from '@/app/utils' 128 129 export default async function Page() { 130 // Read request data before `after` — this is allowed 131 // These calls will be read during the component's rendering lifecycle 132 const userAgent = (await headers()).get('user-agent') || 'unknown' 133 const sessionCookie = 134 (await cookies()).get('session-id')?.value || 'anonymous' 135 136 after(() => { 137 // Use the values read above 138 logUserAction({ sessionCookie, userAgent }) 139 }) 140 141 return

My Page

142 } 143 ``` 144 145 ```jsx filename="app/page.jsx" highlight={8-10,12} switcher 146 import { after } from 'next/server' 147 import { cookies, headers } from 'next/headers' 148 import { logUserAction } from '@/app/utils' 149 150 export default async function Page() { 151 // Read request data before `after` — this is allowed 152 // These calls will be read during the component's rendering lifecycle 153 const userAgent = (await headers()).get('user-agent') || 'unknown' 154 const sessionCookie = 155 (await cookies()).get('session-id')?.value || 'anonymous' 156 157 after(() => { 158 // Use the values read above 159 logUserAction({ sessionCookie, userAgent }) 160 }) 161 162 return

My Page

163 } 164 ``` 165 166 Calling `cookies()` or `headers()` inside the `after` callback in a Server Component will throw a runtime error. 167 168 #### With Cache Components 169 170 When using [Cache Components](/docs/app/getting-started/caching), components that access request data like `cookies` or `headers` must be wrapped in [``](https://react.dev/reference/react/Suspense) so the rest of the page can be prerendered into a static shell. 171 172 You can combine this pattern with `after` by reading request data in a dynamic component and passing it into `after`: 173 174 ```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" highlight={18-19,22-24} switcher 175 import { Suspense } from 'react' 176 import { after } from 'next/server' 177 import { cookies } from 'next/headers' 178 import { logUserAction } from '@/app/utils' 179 180 export default function Page() { 181 return ( 182 <> 183

Part of the static shell

184 Loading...

}> 185 186 187 188 ) 189 } 190 191 async function DynamicContent() { 192 const sessionCookie = 193 (await cookies()).get('session-id')?.value || 'anonymous' 194 195 // Schedule work after the response is sent 196 after(() => { 197 logUserAction({ sessionCookie }) 198 }) 199 200 return

Your session: {sessionCookie}

201 } 202 ``` 203 204 ```jsx filename="app/page.jsx" highlight={18-19,22-24} switcher 205 import { Suspense } from 'react' 206 import { after } from 'next/server' 207 import { cookies } from 'next/headers' 208 import { logUserAction } from '@/app/utils' 209 210 export default function Page() { 211 return ( 212 <> 213

Part of the static shell

214 Loading...

}> 215 216 217 218 ) 219 } 220 221 async function DynamicContent() { 222 const sessionCookie = 223 (await cookies()).get('session-id')?.value || 'anonymous' 224 225 // Schedule work after the response is sent 226 after(() => { 227 logUserAction({ sessionCookie }) 228 }) 229 230 return

Your session: {sessionCookie}

231 } 232 ``` 233 234 In this example, `

` and the `` fallback are included in the static shell. `DynamicContent` reads the cookie during rendering and passes it into `after` via closure. Since `cookies()` is called _outside_ the `after` callback (during the component's render), this works correctly. 235 236 ## Platform Support 237 238 | Deployment Option | Supported | 239 | ------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------- | 240 | [Node.js server](/docs/app/getting-started/deploying#nodejs-server) | Yes | 241 | [Docker container](/docs/app/getting-started/deploying#docker) | Yes | 242 | [Static export](/docs/app/getting-started/deploying#static-export) | No | 243 | [Adapters](/docs/app/getting-started/deploying#adapters) | Platform-specific | 244 245 Learn how to [configure `after`](/docs/app/guides/self-hosting#after) when self-hosting Next.js. 246 247
248 Reference: supporting `after` for serverless platforms 249 250 Using `after` in a serverless context requires waiting for asynchronous tasks to finish after the response has been sent. In Next.js and Vercel, this is achieved using a primitive called `waitUntil(promise)`, which extends the lifetime of a serverless invocation until all promises passed to [`waitUntil`](https://vercel.com/docs/functions/functions-api-reference#waituntil) have settled. 251 252 If you want your users to be able to run `after`, you will have to provide your implementation of `waitUntil` that behaves in an analogous way. 253 254 When `after` is called, Next.js will access `waitUntil` like this: 255 256 ```jsx 257 const RequestContext = globalThis[Symbol.for('@next/request-context')] 258 const contextValue = RequestContext?.get() 259 const waitUntil = contextValue?.waitUntil 260 ``` 261 262 Which means that `globalThis[Symbol.for('@next/request-context')]` is expected to contain an object like this: 263 264 ```tsx 265 type NextRequestContext = { 266 get(): NextRequestContextValue | undefined 267 } 268 269 type NextRequestContextValue = { 270 waitUntil?: (promise: Promise) => void 271 } 272 ``` 273 274 Here is an example of the implementation. 275 276 ```tsx 277 import { AsyncLocalStorage } from 'node:async_hooks' 278 279 const RequestContextStorage = new AsyncLocalStorage() 280 281 // Define and inject the accessor that next.js will use 282 const RequestContext: NextRequestContext = { 283 get() { 284 return RequestContextStorage.getStore() 285 }, 286 } 287 globalThis[Symbol.for('@next/request-context')] = RequestContext 288 289 const handler = (req, res) => { 290 const contextValue = { waitUntil: YOUR_WAITUNTIL } 291 // Provide the value 292 return RequestContextStorage.run(contextValue, () => nextJsHandler(req, res)) 293 } 294 ``` 295 296
297 298 ## Version History 299 300 | Version | Changes | 301 | ------------ | ---------------------------- | 302 | `v15.1.0` | `after` became stable. | 303 | `v15.0.0-rc` | `unstable_after` introduced. | 304

content.content: node_modules/next/dist/esm/server/web/exports/index.js:7:export { after } from '../../after';

content.text: Confirmed. `after` is exported from `next/server`. The bug is that `resend.emails.send(...).catch(console.error)` is fire-and-forget — in this Next.js version the request lifecycle can be torn down right after the response is returned, so the email send may never actually complete. I'll wrap it in `after()` so it's guaranteed to run to completion.

content.text:
Fixed. The Resend call itself was correct (`resend.emails.send` is the right API for `resend@6.12.4`, and `RESEND_API_KEY` is set), but it was fired with `.catch(console.error)` and never awaited — in this Next.js version (16.2.7) the request execution context can be torn down immediately after the response is returned, so an un-awaited promise can be killed before the email actually sends.

Fix in `src/app/api/auth/signup/route.ts`:
- Imported `after` from `next/server`.
- Wrapped the `resend.emails.send(...)` call in `after(async () => {...})` with try/catch — this is the documented pattern for non-blocking work that must still run to completion after the response is sent, while keeping signup itself fast and resilient to email failures.

Type check passes. Note: I didn't run an end-to-end signup test since that would create a real Supabase user and send a live email — let me know if you'd like me to verify that way.
